[RISH BOOK LOVER &

Vor. I APRIL, 1910. No. 9
THE FERGUSON CENTENARY. L
The Centenary of Sir Samuel Ferguson was celebrated with  much %

' enthusiasm in Belfast, the city of his birth. The proceedings com-

' menced on Wednesday, 9th of March, when Miss E. Alexander opened ’ afes
in the Art Gallery, an exhibition of relics and mementoes of Ferguson _ b
and his illustrious fellow-workers in art, literature, music and archso- , ~*
logy, and gave a delightful address. This was followed by a reception __'
by the Lord Mayor, McMordie. Sir John Byers delivered an interesting | i_ ‘ ;
lecture on IFerguson’s life and work to the students of Victoria College, e
and the Ulster Literary Theatre produced his dramatic scene ‘“ The 1 1]
Naming of Cuchullain.” On Thursday, 10th, Mr. F. J. Bigger placed i,l,
a wreath of bay upon the graves of Ferguson and his wife in Donegore, i :,‘:j
and after a memorial service in the church, delivered a glowing eulo- R el
gium on their beautiful lives. | 5*%3’;1

In the evening Mr. Percwal Graves interested a crowded audience 2 i g
with the following centenary address:—The relation in wliich SN !*
Sir Samuel Ferguson stood towards Irish music and poetry S { ”"
¥as remarkable. As a poet, born both before and after his time, he e |
Wwas the only modern Irish epic writer of commandmg genius who - = ;'if-‘. "

united in his personality the three requirements of the old Irish bard——- SRR e i
Lips free from satire’s poisonous flow, e |

Knowledge that nothing base doth L.now }

And love unsullied as the snow. = S S :

Yet whlle Lhe three ancient sorrows of Irish story-tellmg——the fate of e
the Children of Lir, the fate of the Sons of Tuu:eann ‘and the fate of the e
ons of Uisneach—are written in sta:tely Gwehc prose, Ferguson Sy
Modelled himself upon Homer, but in the metre ot Ohapman S great B :

r!3imssla.i-.1c:u1 when he gave himself over to his magnum opus Congal et ‘ " 7

| iﬂnlt grent @Dlo records the ﬁnal struggle between pagamsm and Ghrlst.-
» In which the legions of the Cross ultlmately preva.ll on the ﬁeld ‘ t
tugh Ralh Thls poem abounds with great passages, and, whﬂst. "j_:';_:;:.-.i' I

114 " THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

as Roden Noel remarks, Ferguson frequently makes use/ of the sonor-
ous native names they are sometimes almost too thickly strewn over
his pages, but the interest on the whole is sustained throughout, and
the development of the action is conducted with the utmost artistie
skill ; the chariot of song thunders majestically to its goal with burning
axle in direct impetuous course. But Ferguson Is even greater in the
epopee than in the epic. The “Tain Quest,”” written 1n rhymed
trochaics, had the sublime and impetuous power and a mystical
grandeur unequalled in any modern poem written upon an heroic super-
natural subject. For splendid savagery KFerguson’s “Welshmen of
Tirawley ” has excited the admiration of Swinburne, and for a calm
beauty of an old world character * Aideen’s Grave ’’ furnished a beau-
tiful contrast. Indeed, the modes of Ferguson’s Heroic Lyric were
many and wonderful; but he achieved his highest excellence in his
great dramatic poem of *“ Conary,” which Mr. W. B. Yeaits placed even
before his vivid and haunting ¢ Deirdre.”” Ferguson did not, like
some of his successors, reproduce the verse technique of the old Irish
bards exhibited by Dr.Douglas Hyde and others in their works on
Irish Gaelic metres.. He struck out a line of his own, and a strong and
significant one, of which perhaps the most original was to be found in
the ‘““Welshman of Tirawley.” It was only when he was translating
from the Irish that Ferguson felt himself constrained to adopt Gaelic
measures. Ferguson had made up his mind to turn his back upon
modern ithemes, or at any rate only to give them a by-the-way attention.
He was persuaded of the nobility of heroic Trish subjects. He was
equally persuaded that at the time they had no public behind them.
A generation before, they would have had the support of a cultured and
unprovincialised upper class; a generation later they would have
claimed attention in his hands as the noblest outcome of the Irish
literary revival. He was therefore both before and after his time, and
he rgalised his position to the full. “ Tndeed,” said the lecturel
““when I once spoke to him, with regrat, of the neglect of all but Trish
political literature, he acknowledged it but with a quiet expression of

~ confidence that his time would come.” It has come to-day! Quite
‘apart from the beautiful addresses delivered on the previous day bY
thg~...1i,bérgry.'daughter of famous literary parents, Miss Alexander, theré
~ appears in the March number of the ““Irish Monthly ™ a masterly I
wiew of Ferguson’s poetry, which was delivered in tha form of & 0%

SR Aure for the Irish Literary Bociety of London by the FHon. Roden Noek:

lus, interpreting the mystic scenes and sounds of nature, primeval
forest, wild craggy mountain or stormy ocean.” Miss Alexander and
Miss Hickey had shown the fine appreciation of Irish-women’s liter-
ary insight into Ferguson’s position as an Irish poet. To the testi-
mony of Professor Dowden that Ferguson was the only true epic poet of
the Victorian age, he wished to add the following appreciation which
he had received that morning from Stopford Brooke, the foremost liv-
Ing literary critic who wrote in the English language:—“I have not | s
only a great admiration for Sir Samuel Ferguson’s poems, but I con- '

Sider him as the first and perhaps the best of all those who have -
striven to bring into recognition, light, and beauty the ancient poetic i
Sagas and tales of Ireland. The scholars have done much to make :
them known; he struck home to their poetry, and fired into import- e
fince their national essence, and great has been the use and the results nene g }
of this to the Irish people and to the literature of the world. What aoe il
he did so well, and with such fire and passion has had a life in it

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 115 g
Irish on his mother’s side and a poet who had yet to win the fuller | el
recognition due to his genius. This appreciation was followed by a
charming poetic tribute to Ferguson by Miss Emily Hickey, in whose
hands the manuscript of this lecture had been lett. He (the lecturer) :
did not think the characteristics of Ferguson’s genius could be more :
ably summed up than in this fine passage from that criticism by g
Roden Noel:— But the poetry of Ferguson is the very reverse of what 1 :
may be described as popular; it is human, unaffected, sincere, without (]|
mannerism, remarkable for economy of power and parsimony of epi- | i
thet—concentrated, firm in outline, with a strong grip upon the chosen oo
theme; vivid and graphic in delineation; constructed with the utmost T 45
artistic skill, symmetrical proportion of parts, and general unity of '
efiect; now and again moving us profoundly by a touch of unforced and i } >
thrilling pathos all the more telling for this economy of power and use
of the simplest means to produce the effect intended. A touch does it, ; * =
a master touch as in Byron’s ‘ Gladiator’ or Wordsworth’s “Michael,.’ i
Or a song of Burns’s, or one of the old ballads such as the ‘ Twa Cor- A
bies.” Sometimes profoundly tragic, and again instinct with the fairy r
glamour or shadowy sublimities of the early Celts—mythopoetic gen- : *‘i

Which hag grown and will continue in the work of others.”’

-~ (To be continued.) s S SRR R SR

r= ‘ SINGLETON’S SECRET PRESS—WHIERI?

“'L: In our third number Mr. H. R. Plomer, author of * The History
of Piinting,” in an article on * Ireland and Secret! Printing ™ (p. 27),

threw out the suggestion that some of the many controversial tracts
of the sixteenth century may have been printed in Ireland, though

bearing other imprints. “ The Mysterious press of Hugh Singleton,

* for example,” he said, ‘“has never yet been located.” Since then
Mr. Plomer has been following up the scent, and he gives the result
of his prolonged researches in the current number of *‘ The Library”
"1 (Alex. Moring, Ltd.) Irom a careful examination of the type used in
3 these works, he divides them into three classes, one of which he desig-
‘“ nates the  Waterford ’type, which was that used In printing *° The

Acquital or Purgation oi the Mooste Catholycke Christen Prince Ed-
 warde the VI.” a facsimile of the colophon of which is-here given.

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 117

No less than twelve books issued in the years 1555 and 1556 are
found printed with this type. The title-pages of the Waterford books B
have a character of their own. The first line is almost always in lower e
case roman, followed by a line or two of large German script, the rest
being in one or other of the numerous founts the office possessed; B |
whilst the water-marks on the paper is sometimes a hand and star, and | e
sometimes a ““pot.”” Cotton in his “ Typographical Gazetteer,” 2nd
series, mentions three books in the Waterford type, viz.: The one e
mentioned above, Scory’s ‘“ Epistle to Faythfull,” and Cranmer’s ‘“ Con- '
futations of Unwritten Verities,” giving as his authority for the last,
the catalogue of T.C.D. Library, where a copy that has since disap- L
peared, was entered as having the Waterford imprint. The copy of I ot
this work in the British Museum is without date and the type used e
is not the Waterford. Mr. Plomer adduces much convincing evidence B
that these books issued from one office, the work of one printer—Hugh | g
Singleton, and asks “ Where was that office?”’ a question he proceeds = :?‘
to answer as well as he can as follows: Hugh Singleton is first heard of 5
in 1548 when he issued several books with his own imprint, and one RiK
was printed for him by Humphrey Powell, which shows an early busi- |
ness relationship between the two. It is also a singular coincidence
that neither printed anything else with a date until some years later.
There is also a striking resemblance, not only in the types, but in the SRR
press work of the two men. In 1550 Powell gave up business in Lon- S
don, went to Dublin with his type, and there pr inted “° The Book of" i
Common Prayer’ in 1551, the first book printed in Ireland. In 1553
Singleton’s press 18 again active, and thr ce small things came from 1t,
but after this, it is silent throughout the remainder of 1 ‘\fIary S re1gn._
Another singular thing is that Powell’s press was also apparently
silent from 1551 to 1561. It is hardly conceivable that both men were
idle during the reign of Mary, and equally unconceivable - that this
Secret press was worked in London, thd risk of discovery would have-
been too great. But if Powell could go to Ireland, why not Smgleton?
The business relationship between the two has already been proved, -
and Powell’s office in® Dublin was quite as safe as any place on the
Continent, for carrying on a secret press, and there is nothing 1mpreb—

8ble in: the suggestion that Singleton may have gone over to Ireland 2

taking with him a stock of foreign letter and initials. A st,ra‘j;};j;;:_;-‘:_{_;‘._;"’;j‘:‘"' :
enough to show which way the wind is blowing, and there erez"@n

118 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

One of these is that in the books found printed by Powell and Single-
ton in 1548, the water-mark on the paper is either a hand and star ora
pot. Again iv the octavos printed by Powell in 1548 he signed five
leaves ofi the sheet, and this will be found to be a feature of these
secretly printed books. Finally both Powell and Singleton were in the
habit of printing the first line of the text in heavier type than the rest
which is also characteristic of the books under notice.

In taking leave for the present of this most interesting subject we
have to acknowledge the courtesy of Mr. Plomer, the author, and Mr,
Pollard, the editor, for allowing us free use of the article, and Messrs.
Moring, the publishers, for the loan of the block.

SMITH O’BRIEN’S PAMPHLETS.

I knew William Smith O’Brien. My first interview with him was
at 107, Stephen’s Green, where he called upon me, the day he returned

to Ireland, from his exile, to discuss some educational subject. He

; l collected Irish pamphlets, but only the best—those referring to the
progresé of Ireland, or to religious subjects of the pure, generous, and

_.i : exalted tendency, which his mother loved and taught him. He had

< ’ some of them bound up in three vols. for presentation to her. Nearly

: - S all of them hore an inscription, such as “To my beloved mother,

W. S. O’B.” His handwriting in these pamphlets and in his letters
was beautiful. A few days after his arrest and incarceration in Rich-

- mond prison, he received a present of a box of Burren Oysters from a
lady admirer. I have his letter of thanks to the sender. It is beauti-
;f";,;‘-_f;; ful, and will be sold with my collection of autographs, at Sotheby’s

‘ ghortly.

One of the presentation pamphlets was entitled * Nirwana, or 8
Dream,” a very rare and beautiful production, originally written by
Q. Q. (Jane Taylor), and dealing with the Eastern ideas of the Suprein®
vLBemg Another was the rarest pamphlet I have seen on the Irish
Famine. It is entitled:. ‘“ Narrative of a Journey from Oxford 10
Skibbereen during the year of the Irish Famine”’ (8vo., pp. 28, Oxford,
1847.) It was written by the late Lord Dufferin when he was only

_ twenty years old. He asked for fourteen days’ leave from Oxford, and
""_;.havmg got it, proceeded to Dublin with his friend the Hon. George
P Yl Boyle, afterwards Earl of Glasgow. He borrowed and begged amongst
B 1ig ﬁxends and relations, and walked with Boyle to Athy, and then

2 4
~E o "’a‘-‘f'm-

Y oli eoach to Sklbbereen. I was a boy at the time, and well remember

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 119 |
peing sent 1o Skibbdreen on business, and well remember seeing
Dufferin and Boyle, with the Doctor, the priest, the parson and his wife, ,
all working hard in burying the dead! They stayed a few days and !
got back to Oxford on the evening of the fourteenth day. They stayea I
up all night whilst the pamphlet was being printed. Copies were sent ‘ L
to the Prime Minister and members of ithe Cabinet; to the rich and ’
charitable, and money poured in, which was at once transmitted to :
Skibbereen. After this good act, Lord Dufferin was recognised as one S |
of the best of men. I lent my unique copy inscribed as above ‘‘To '
my beloved mother. W. S. O’B,” to Sir Alfred Lyall when he was writ- £
ing the “ Life of Lord Dufferin.” Ha

| His 18th century pamphlets dealing with Irish parliamentary
affairs, I found very good, and very rare, as I was only able to get two %
volumes of them. I have a large stock of these Irish pamphlets, many 1 B g,;f g
of them rare. Pamphlets in 18th and 19th centuries were the political i f“"
educators of the time, and should be preserved, as they are more im- L e
pressive, to my mind, than the general histories. ;
Ennis. JAMES HAYES, M.R.S.A I
(We print Mr. Hayes’ very interesting communication with. much i 8 1
pleasure, and trust he will favour us with further recollections.—Ed.) ".1-"

o

GOSSIP.

The fine philological library, collected by the late Whitley Stokes, -

the well-known Celtic scholar, has been presented by his daughters to
| the London University. R ~

Professor Yelverton, Tyrell, of Trinity, whilom, “‘editor of Kotta
bos,” and prince of good fellows,” as one of his contributors dubbed
him, has issued through-the house of Macmillan a volume of Essays
on Greek Literature,”” which will add to his already great reputation
88 a classical scholar. His irrepressible humour bubbles over here
&fld there, as in the article on Plutarch, where he quotes this bit of
dialogue overheard not long ago in an Irish county: ‘“And would
?hﬁ take the poor boy's life for the like o’ that? Bedad, they would,
it he had as many lives as Plutarch !” -
o s Some time ago, it was stated that Messrs. Ouseley, whose New
'“rlﬂ.h Library has been a success, had in preparation an authoritative
| Life of John Redmond, M.P.,”” but now they announce, that in defer- =
:3“‘3 to the wish of the Irish Leader, expressed in an interview, their
t  THingement with the author—name not mentioned, has been cancelled.

FORTHCOMING BOOKS.

Our Irish writers have been busy with a view to the Spring pub-
lishing season, and amongst the books to be looked for are Mrs. Blun-
dell’s (M. E. Francis) “ The Wild Heart,” and ° Betty Carew,” by
Katherine Tynan, both from Smith and Elder. Mr. Frankfort Mocre’s
“ The Laird: of Craig Athol” is promised by Constables. Mr. Murray
will publish in April, Mr. DeVere Stackpoole’s “ Drums of War,” a
story of the Franco-Prussian Campaign of 1870, and Mr. G. A. Bir-
mingham will contribute to a new series proposed by Nelson’s to contain
original} fiction at the price oi two shillings net, which will probably
be the death-blow of the six shilling novel. Mus. Thurston’s “ Max”
is due srom Hutchinson’s, and Justin Huntley McCarthy 1s publishing
with Hurst and Blackett, the story of ¢ The O’Flynn,” with which Sir
Herbert Tree has been drawing enthusiastic audiences to His Majesty's
Theatre during the past month.

Turning to more serious—though not less entertaining literature,

" Messrs. Methuen announce ‘“ A History of the Speakers of the House

of Commons,” by _Mi;ch_ael MacDonagh, who knows Parliament outside
and in, and a work entitled “ Famous Bluestockings,” from the pen of
Miss Ethel Rolt Wheeler, a gifted member of the Irish Literary Soci_etY-

BonymaroN.—Though Bonmahon is chiefly known nowadays for the ‘ b
copper mines in its vicinity, at present not worked, there is a still :

earlier literary association connected with it than that derived from :
the Rev. Doudney’s press. Philip F. Barron, a Waterford gentleman,. R

who was a great enthusiast in regard to the Irish language, built &
small college about a mile from Bonmahon for the purpose of promoting 2

its study. He also founded a magazine with the same object, called
““Ancient Ireland,” whose first number was published at Dublin on
the 1st of January, 1835, of which only five numbers were issued. Of
this Irish college the foundations were barely traceable some fifteen:
years ago; but of Philip Barron, its founder, no memoir has ever been:

F. J. Bicger (vir. p. 101).—" The Schoolmaster’s Magazine and
Educational Inquirer,” was published at Armagh monthly by John
McWaters. Volume I. is in Trinity College Library (Shelf mark/54 u.
9.) It is a large 8vo. of 12 parts and contains 384 pp. No. 3 was prin-

I ted by John Henry, City Press, 3, Russell Streeti, Armagh, and Nos.
5 and 6 by Joseph Smyth, of 34, High Street, Beliast. There 18 no
printers name given for the other Nos., but John McWaters was him-

self a printer in Armagh. E. R. Mc. C. DIX,

The Presbyterian Historical Society, Belfast, has a copy of the first
volume of the ¢ Schoolmaster’s Magazine and Educational Inquirer.”’
It runs from September, 1839, to August, 1840, and was evidently formed
in close connection with the first teachers’ organisation In

The library of Sir Francis W. Brady, Bart., was sold in Dublin on

March 8th. It comprised a number of important works, for which
competition was not remarkably keen. A bound set of the “ Annals of
the Kingdom of Ireland,” (original subscriber’s copy of the 1847-51 edi-
tion), realised only £10. The first eight volumes of the first series of the
Ulster Archaological Journal forn at some pages, brought £4 12s. 6q.
0 Donovan’s ““ Tribes and Customs of Hy. Many ” commanded 398.,,

“ Cambrensis Eversus,” 3 vols., only 20s., Gilbert’s History of Dub-
. lin,” brought 85s., D’Alton’s “ King James’s Irish Army List (secon N Sy
edition), 23s., his ““ History of Drogheda,” 16s., and two ccmplé’ce; s

- IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY.

1 he Original Night held on Saturday, March 5th, took the form of
a Celebration of the Centenary of Sir Samuel Ferguson. Mr. A. P.
Graves opened the proceedings with an oration on the life and work
of Ferguson. Miss May Coleman sang the ““ Lark in Clear Air,” to her
own accompaniment on the knee harp. Mr. Hugh Law, M.P., rendered
Ferguson’s poem, ¢« The Burial of King Cormac,” with remarkable
force. Musical items were contributed by Mrs. Milligan Fox, Miss
Georgina Macdonald (harp), Miss Edith Kirkwood, Miss May Mukle,
Miss Carrie Townshend, and Mr. Owen Colyer. Recitations were given
by Miss Anne Mather and Miss Florence Farr. Mr. Joseph Campbell
recited Ferguson’s translation from the Irish ‘“ The Fair Hills of Ire-
land,” the Gaelic refrain “ Uileacan dubh O!” being chanted in
chorus with excellent effect. ““ The Naming of Cuchullain’ was read,
the different parts being taken by Hugh Law, M.P., Miss Mabel Dick-

- ipson, Mr. Lane, Mr. J. R. Monsell, and Mr. J. Campbell.

The audience was very large, and the excellent programme Was

‘much appreciated.

A -Hisbo"ry Night was held on Thursday, 10th, when Miss M. San-

dars read a paper on ‘““A Frenchman in Ireland in 1690.”” Mr. W. H.

-M_agcMahon was i_n the chair, Miss Sandars is a well-known autho;'it)'
on French history, and her paper dealt with the experiences and 1m-
pressiong of Gen. Lauzun, during the war in Ireland, as recorded in

his diary. Miss Sandars showed how doubtful were the advantages
- which Ireland derived from French aid and refuted their slanders I¢
- garding the behaviour of the Irish troops at the Boyne. A hearty ¥

ote

of thanks was passed on the motion of Mr. J. Buckley, M.R.L.A., B¢

SOME RECENT OPINIONS OF THE PRESS.

«« THE ATHENZAUM ” says:—

« THE IRISH BOOK LOVER,” No. VII. of which is before
ug, gives various notes, reviews, and comments concerning Irish
books. We observe also a section of queries, and the beginnings of
an Irish bibliography. THE WHOLE IS WELL DONE, and evi-
dently the work of an enthusiast.” '

THE JOURNAL OF THE WATERFORD ARCHAOLOGICAL
SOCIETY says:—‘ The Irish Book Lover »* has come to hand, and now
that we have a periodical of this special character, we are inclinad to
wonder how we have been able to do so long without it! The “ Roak
Lover >’ is indeed excellent in design, scope and achievement.

« THE DUBLIN EVENING TELEGRAPH " says:—

« THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.” The gossip, the reviews,
literary notes, and answers to queries are full of varied, interesting,
and out-of-the-way information on a variety of topics that appeal
to Itish book lovers. An excellent feature is the quarterly supple-
ment of Irish bibliography. The current number of this interest-
ing publication shows a great advance on'the initial issues.”

« THE WESTMEATH GUARDIAN ” says:—

« "HE IRISH BOOK LOVER.” ‘ The above interesting pub-
lication has reached its sixth number, and on looking through the
current issue and previous ones, we feel bound to say that it does
infinite credit to its editor, Dr. J. 8. Crone, and the circle of Irish
literary workers who are associated with it. It is devoted to Trish

’bibliOgraphy, and has been established to serve as a medium of

inter-communication for all those interested in books relating ¥

Treland. In dealing with Irish typography it has recently pub*

lished a series of interesting articles on Irish printing presses |

Already ‘ The Irish Book Lover’ has a world-wide circulation:

is supplied for the merely nominal sum of two shillings per ye&”
